content: If $$x = {e^t}\sin t$$, $$y = {e^t}\cos t$$ then $${{{d^2}y} \over {d{x^2}}}$$ at x = $$\pi$$ is
explanation:
$$x = {e^t}\sin t$$, $$y = {e^t}\cos t$$

$$\therefore$$ $${{dx} \over {dt}} = {d \over {dt}}({e^t}\sin t) = {e^t}{d \over {dt}}\sin t + \sin t{d \over {dt}}{e^t}$$

$$ = {e^t}\cos t + \sin t({e^t})$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{dx} \over {dt}} = {e^t}(\cos t + \sin t)$$ ..... (i)

Similarly, $${{dy} \over {dt}} = {d \over {dt}}{e^t}\cos t = {e^t}{d \over {dt}}\cos t + \cos t.{d \over {dt}}{e^t}$$

$$ = {e^t}( - \sin t) + \cos t({e^t}) = {e^t}(\cos t - \sin t)$$ ...... (ii)

$$\therefore$$ $${{dy} \over {dx}} = {{dy/dt} \over {dx/dt}} = {{{e^t}(\cos t - \sin t)} \over {{e^t}(\cos t + \sin t)}} = {{\cos t - \sin t} \over {\cos t + \sin t}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${{{d^2}y} \over {d{x^2}}} = {d \over {dx}}\left( {{{dy} \over {dx}}} \right) = {d \over {dx}}\left( {{{\cos t - \sin t} \over {\cos t + \sin t}}} \right) = {d \over {dt}}\left( {{{\cos t - \sin t} \over {\cos t + \sin t}}} \right).{{dt} \over {dx}}$$

$$ = {{(\cos t + \sin t)( - \sin t - \cos t) - (\cos t - \sin t)(\cos t - \sin t)} \over {{{(\cos t + \sin t)}^2}}}.{{dt} \over {dx}}$$

$$ = {{ - {{(\cos t + \sin t)}^2} - {{(\cos t - \sin t)}^2}} \over {{{(\cos t + \sin t)}^2}}}.{1 \over {{e^t}(\cos t + \sin t)}}$$ [$$\because$$ $${{dt} \over {dx}} = {1 \over {dx/dt}}$$]

$$\therefore$$ $${\left( {{{{d^2}y} \over {d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = \pi }} = {{ - {{(\cos \pi + \sin \pi )}^2} - {{(\cos \pi - \sin \pi )}^2}} \over {{{(\cos \pi + \sin \pi )}^2}}}.{1 \over {{e^\pi }(\cos \pi + \sin \pi )}}$$

$$ = {{ - 1 - 1} \over {{{( - 1)}^2}}} \times {1 \over {{e^\pi }( - 1)}} = {2 \over {{e^\pi }}}$$.

content: The value of $${{dy} \over {dx}}$$ at $$x = {\pi \over 2}$$, where y is given by $$y = {x^{\sin x}} + \sqrt x $$ is
explanation:
$$y = {x^{\sin x}} + \sqrt x $$

Let $$y = u + v$$

$$\therefore$$ $$u = {x^{\sin x}}$$

Taking log on both sides, $$\log u = \sin x\log x$$

Taking derivative w.r.t. x

$${1 \over u}{{du} \over {dx}} = {{\sin x} \over x} + \cos x\log x$$

$${{du} \over {dx}} = u\left( {{{\sin x} \over x} + \cos x\log x} \right)$$ ..... (i)

$$v = \sqrt x $$, $${{dv} \over {dx}} = {1 \over {2\sqrt x }}$$ ...... (ii)

Since, $$y = u + v$$

$${{dy} \over {dx}} = {{du} \over {dx}} + {{dv} \over {dx}}$$

$${{dy} \over {dx}} = {x^{\sin x}}\left( {{{\sin x} \over x} + \cos x\log x} \right) + {1 \over {2\sqrt x }}$$ (from (i) and (ii))

$${\left( {{{dy} \over {dx}}} \right)_{at\,x = \pi /2}} = {\pi \over 2}\left( {{2 \over \pi }} \right) + {{1 \times \sqrt 2 } \over {2\sqrt \pi }} = 1 + {1 \over {\sqrt {2\pi } }}$$.

content: Select the correct statement from (a), (b), (c), (d). The function $$f(x) = x{e^{1 - x}}$$
explanation:
$$f(x) = x{e^{1 - x}}$$

$$f'(x) = x{d \over {dx}}{e^{1 - x}} + {e^{(1 - x)}}{d \over {dx}}x$$

$$ = - x{e^{1 - x}} + {e^{1 - x}} = (1 - x){e^{1 - x}}$$

If $$f'(x) < 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (1 - x){e^{1 - x}} < 0$$ ($$\because$$ $${e^{1 - x}} > 0$$)

$$ \Rightarrow 1 - x < 0 \Rightarrow x > 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow x \in (1,\infty )$$ so function is strictly decreasing.

content: The function $$f(x) = {e^{ax}} + {e^{ - ax}},a > 0$$ is monotonically increasing for
explanation:
$$f(x) = {e^{ax}} + {e^{ - ax}}$$

$$f'(x) = a{e^{ax}} - a{e^{ - ax}} = a({e^{ax}} - {e^{ - ax}})$$

If f(x) is monotonically increasing

then $$f'(x) > 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow a({e^{ax}} - {e^{ - ax}}) > 0 \Rightarrow ({e^{ax}} - {e^{ - ax}}) > 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {e^{ax}} > {e^{ - ax}} \Rightarrow {e^{2ax}} > 1 = {e^0}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2ax > 0 \Rightarrow x > 0$$ ($$because$$ $$a > 0$$)

$$\therefore$$ function f(x) is increasing when x > 0.

content: The second order derivative of a sin3t with respect to a cos3t at $$t = {\pi \over 4}$$ is
If $$y = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\sqrt {{{1 - \sin x} \over {1 + \sin x}}} $$, then the value of $${{dy} \over {dx}}$$ at $$x = {\pi \over 6}$$ is
explanation:
$$y = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\sqrt {{{1 - \sin x} \over {1 + \sin x}}} $$

$$ = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\sqrt {{{{{\cos }^2}{x \over 2} + {{\sin }^2}{x \over 2} - 2\sin {x \over 2}\cos {x \over 2}} \over {{{\cos }^2}{x \over 2} + {{\sin }^2}{x \over 2} + 2\sin {x \over 2}\cos {x \over 2}}}} $$

$$ = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\sqrt {{{{{\left( {\cos {x \over 2} - \sin {x \over 2}} \right)}^2}} \over {{{\left( {\cos {x \over 2} + \sin {x \over 2}} \right)}^2}}}} = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {{{\cos {x \over 2} - \sin {x \over 2}} \over {\cos {x \over 2} + \sin {x \over 2}}}} \right)$$ ($${x \over 2} = 15^\circ $$ $$\therefore$$ $$\cos {x \over 2} > \sin {x \over 2}$$)

$$ = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {{{1 - \tan {x \over 2}} \over {1 + \tan {x \over 2}}}} \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left[ {\tan \left( {{\pi \over 4} - {x \over 2}} \right)} \right] = {\pi \over 4} - {\pi \over 2}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${{dy} \over {dx}} = - {1 \over 2}$$

content: If x2 + y2 = 4, then $$y{{dy} \over {dx}} + x = $$
explanation: $$\because$$ $${x^2} + {y^2} = 4$$ $$\therefore$$ $$2x + 2y{{dy} \over {dx}} = 0 \Rightarrow y{{dy} \over {dx}} + x = 0$$

content: Let $$f(x) = ta{n^{ - 1}}x$$. Then $$f'(x) + f''(x)=0$$, when x is equal to

explanation:
$$y = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {{{\sqrt {1 + {x^2}} - 1} \over x}} \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {{{\sqrt {1 + {{\tan }^2}\theta } - 1} \over {\tan \theta }}} \right)$$ [putting $$x = \tan \theta $$]

$$ = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {{{\sec \theta - 1} \over {\tan \theta }}} \right) = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {{{2{{\sin }^2}{\theta \over 2}} \over {2\sin {\theta \over 2}\cos {\theta \over 2}}}} \right) = {\theta \over 2} = {1 \over 2}{\tan ^{ - 1}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$y' = {1 \over {2(1 + {x^2})}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$y'(1) = {1 \over 4}$$

content: If $$y = 2{x^3} - 2{x^2} + 3x - 5$$, then for x = 2 and $$\Delta$$x = 0.1 the value of $$\Delta$$y is
The approximate value of $$\root 5 \of {33} $$ correct to 4 decimal places is
explanation:
$$\left( {{0 \over 0}} \right)$$

$$\because$$ $$f(x + \Delta x) - f(x) = f'(x)\,.\,\Delta x$$ ...... (i)

Putting x = 32, $$\Delta$$x = 1

$$\Rightarrow$$ x = 1, $$ - {3 \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \root 5 \of {33} - \root 5 \of {32} = {1 \over {80}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\root 5 \of {33} = 2.0125$$.

content: Let $$f(x) = {x^3}{e^{ - 3x}},\,x > 0$$. Then the maximum value of f(x) is
If ' $f$ ' is the inverse function of ' $g$ ' and $g^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{1+x^n}$, then the value of $f^{\prime}(x)$ is
explanation:
We are given that

$f$ is the inverse function of $g$ and $g^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{1+x^n}$.

Using the formula for the derivative of an inverse function,

$$ f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{g^{\prime}(f(x))} $$

Substitute the given expression for $g^{\prime}(x)$ into the formula.

$$ g^{\prime}(f(x))=\frac{1}{1+(f(x))^n} $$

therefore,

$$ f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{\frac{1}{1+(f(x))^n}}=1+(f(x))^n $$

content: Let $f(x)$ be a second degree polynomial. If $f(1)=f(-1)$ and $p, q, r$ are in A.P., then $f^{\prime}(p), f^{\prime}(q), f^{\prime}(r)$ are

options.content: neither in A.P. or G.P. or H.P.

explanation:
Let $f(x)$ be a second-degree polynomial, so

$$ f(x)=A x^2+B x+C $$

Given

$$ \begin{aligned} & f(1)=f(-1) \\ & \Rightarrow A(1)^2+B(1)+C=A(-1)^2+B(-1)+C \\ & \Rightarrow \quad A+B+C=A-B+C \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 B=0 \Rightarrow B=0 \\ & \text { Thus, } \quad f(x)=A x^2+C \\ & \text { Then, } \quad f^{\prime}(x)=2 A x \\ & \text { Given, } p, q, r \text { are in AP so } 2 q=p+r \\ & \text { Consider } f^{\prime}(p), f^{\prime}(q), f^{\prime}(r) \\ & \qquad \begin{aligned} f^{\prime}(p) & =2 A p \\ f^{\prime}(q) & =2 A q \\ f^{\prime}(r) & =2 A r \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

For these to be in AP, we check if

$$ \begin{aligned} & & 2 f^{\prime}(q) & =f^{\prime}(p)+f^{\prime}(r) \\ & & 2(2 A q) & =2 A p+2 A r \\ \Rightarrow & & 4 A q & =2 A(p+r) \end{aligned} $$

Dividing by 2A (assuming $A \neq 0$, otherwise $f(x)$ is a constant and $f^{\prime}(x)=0$ for all $x$, in which case they are trivially in AP)

$$ 2 q=p+r . $$

This is true since $p, q, r$ and in AP. therefore, $f^{\prime}(p), f^{\prime}(q), f^{\prime}(r)$ are in AP

content: If $$\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{n}}(\mathrm{n}=1,2)$$ denotes the $$\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$$ derivative $$(\mathrm{n}=1,2)$$ of $$\mathrm{U}(x)=\frac{\mathrm{L} x+\mathrm{M}}{x^2-2 \mathrm{~B} x+\mathrm{C}}$$ (L, M, B, C are constants), then $$\mathrm{PU}_2+\mathrm{QU}_1+\mathrm{RU}=0$$, holds for

$$ \text { If } y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\log _e\left(\frac{e}{x^2}\right)}{\log _e\left(e x^2\right)}\right]+\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{3+2 \log _e x}{1-6 \cdot \log _e x}\right] \text {, then } \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}= $$

Suppose $$f:R \to R$$ be given by $$f(x) = \left\{ \matrix{ 1,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\mathrm{if}\,x = 1 \hfill \cr {e^{({x^{10}} - 1)}} + {(x - 1)^2}\sin {1 \over {x - 1}},\,\mathrm{if}\,x \ne 1 \hfill \cr} \right.$$

then
explanation:
$$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} 1, & x=1 \\ e^{\left(x^{10}-1\right)}+(x-1)^2 \sin \left(\frac{1}{x-1}\right), & x \neq 1 \end{array}\right.$$

$$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1} f(x)=e^{1^{10}-1}+(1-1)^2 \times \quad$$ oscillating number between ($$-1$$ and $$1$$)$$=1+0=1=f(1)$$

$$\therefore f(x) \text { is continuous at } f(l)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & f^{\prime}(x)=e^{\left(x^{10}-1\right)} \times 10 x^9+(x-1)^2 \cos \left(\frac{1}{x-1}\right) \\ & \times \frac{-1}{(x-1)^2}+\sin \left(\frac{1}{x-1}\right) \times 2(x-1) \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \lim _{x \rightarrow 1} f^{\prime}(x)=\left(e^{1^{10}-1}\right) \times 10(1)^9+0 \times \text { oscillating number } \\ & \text { between }(-1 \text { and } 1)+0=10 \\ & \therefore \quad f^{\prime}(1) \text { exist and is } 10. \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $${\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {{y \over b}} \right) = {\log _e}{\left( {{x \over n}} \right)^n}$$, then $$A{y_2} + B{y_1} + Cy = 0$$ is possible for, where $${y_2} = {{{d^2}y} \over {d{x^2}}},{y_1} = {{dy} \over {dx}}$$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{b}\right)=\log _e\left(\frac{x}{n}\right)^n=n \ln \left(\frac{x}{n}\right) \\ & =n \ln (x)-n \ln (n) \\ & \quad=\cos \left[\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{b}\right)\right]=\cos (n \ln x-n \ln n) \end{aligned}$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{y}{b}=\cos (n \ln x-n \ln n)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow y=b \cos (n \ln x-n \ln n) \\ & \Rightarrow y_1=-b \sin (n \ln x-n \ln n) \times \frac{n}{x} \end{aligned}$$

$$\Rightarrow y_2=-b n\left[x \times \cos (n \ln x-n \ln n) \times \frac{n}{x}\right. \left.\frac{-\sin (n \ln x-n \ln n)}{x^2}\right]$$

$$=\frac{-b n[n \cos (n \ln x-n \ln n)-\sin (n \ln x-n \ln n)]}{x^2}$$

$$\text { Now, } A y_2+B y_1+C y=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{-A b n[n \cos (n \ln x-n \ln n)-\sin (n \ln x-n \ln n)]}{x^2} \\ & -\frac{B b n \sin (n \ln x-n \ln n)}{x}+C b \cos (n \ln x-n \ln n)=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow-A n^2 \cos (n \ln x-n \ln n) & +A n \sin (n \ln x \\ -n \ln n)-B n x & \sin (n \ln x-n \ln n) \\ & +C x^2 \cos (n \ln x-n \ln n)=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow\left(-A n^2+C x^2\right) & \cos (n \ln x-n \ln n) +(A n-B n x) \sin (n \ln x-n \ln n)=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad-A n^2+C x^2=0 \Rightarrow C x^2=A n^2 \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ & \text { and } \quad A n-B n x=0 \Rightarrow A=B x \quad \text{.... (ii)}\\ & \therefore \quad A=x^2, B=x, C=n^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The function $$y = {e^{kx}}$$ satisfies $$\left( {{{{d^2}y} \over {d{x^2}}} + {{dy} \over {dx}}} \right)\left( {{{dy} \over {dx}} - y} \right) = y{{dy} \over {dx}}$$. It is valid for
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { } y=e^{k x} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=k e^{k x}=k y \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=k^2 e^{k x}=k^2 y \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+\frac{d y}{d x}\right)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)=y \frac{d y}{d x} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left(k^2 y+k y\right)(k y-y)=y \times k y \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left(k^2+k\right)(k-1) y^2=k y^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad k(k+1)(k-1)-k=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad k\left[k^2-1-1\right]=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad k\left(k^2-2\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad k=0, \pm \sqrt{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$y = {\log ^n}x$$, where $${\log ^n}$$ means $${\log _e}{\log _e}{\log _e}\,...$$ (repeated n times), then $$x\log x{\log ^2}x{\log ^3}x\,.....\,{\log ^{n - 1}}x{\log ^n}x{{dy} \over {dx}}$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$y=\underbrace{\log _e \log _e \log _e \ldots \log _e x}_{n \text {-times }}$$

Using chain rule of differentiation,

$$\frac{d y}{d x}=\underbrace{\frac{1}{\log _e \log _e \ldots \log _e x}}_{(n-1) \text { times }} \times \underbrace{\frac{1}{\log _e \ldots \log _e x}}_{(n-2) \text { times }} \times \ldots \times \frac{1}{\log _e \log _e x} \times \frac{1}{\log _e x} \times \frac{1}{x}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\left(\log ^{n-1} x\right)\left(\log ^{n-2} x\right) \ldots\left(\log ^2 x\right)(\log x)(x)} \\ & \Rightarrow x \log x \log ^2 x \ldots \log ^{n-1} x \frac{d y}{d x}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow x \log x \log ^2 x \ldots \log ^{n-1} x \log ^n x \frac{d y}{d x}=\log ^n x \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$x = \sin \theta $$ and $$y = \sin k\theta $$, then $$(1 - {x^2}){y_2} - x{y_1} - \alpha y = 0$$, for $$\alpha=$$
explanation:
We have, $$x=\sin \theta$$ and $$y=\sin k \theta$$

Differentiate $$x$$ and $$y$$ w.r.t. $$\theta$$,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d x}{d \theta}=\cos \theta \text { and } \frac{d y}{d \theta}=k \cos k \theta \\ & y_1=\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d \theta} \times \frac{d \theta}{d x}=\frac{k \cos k \theta}{\cos \theta} \end{aligned}$$

Again, differentiate $$\frac{d y}{d x}$$ w.r.t. $$x$$,

$$\begin{aligned} y_2 & =\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2} \\ & =\frac{\cos \theta\left[-k^2 \sin k \theta\right]-k \cos k \theta(-\sin \theta) }{\cos ^2 \theta}\cdot \frac{d \theta}{d x} \\ & =\frac{k \cos k \theta \sin \theta-k^2 \cos \theta \sin k \theta}{\cos ^3 \theta} \end{aligned}$$

$$\text { Now, }\left(1-x^2\right) y_2-x y_1-\alpha y=0$$

$$\left(1-\sin ^2 \theta\right)\left[\frac{k \cos k \theta \sin \theta-k^2 \cos \theta \sin k \theta}{\cos ^3 \theta}\right] -\sin \theta \times \frac{k \cos k \theta}{\cos \theta}-\alpha \sin k \theta=0$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad k \cos k \theta \sin \theta-k^2 \cos \theta \sin k \theta -k \cos k \theta \sin \theta-\alpha \sin k \theta \cos \theta=0$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \alpha=-k^2$$

$$y = {e^{{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x}}$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get

$$ \Rightarrow {{dy} \over {dx}} = {e^{{{\tan }^{ - 1}}(x)}} \times {1 \over {1 + {x^2}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {y_1}(1 + {x^2}) = {e^{{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {y_1}(1 + {x^2}) = y$$

Differentiating both side with respect to x, we get

$$ \Rightarrow {y_2}(1 + {x^2}) + {y_1}(2x) = {y_1}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {y_2}(1 + {x^2}) + {y_1}(2x - 1) = 0$$

content: Let $$g(x) = \int\limits_x^{2x} {{{f(t)} \over t}dt} $$ where x > 0 and f be continuous function and f(2x) = f(x), then
explanation:
Given,

$$g(x) = \int_x^{2x} {{{f(t)} \over t}dt} $$

and f is a continuous function and $$f(2x) = f(x)$$

As, f is a continuous function so we can apply Leibnitz Rule for differentiation.

$$\therefore$$ $$g'(x) = {d \over {dx}}(2x)\,.\,{{f(2x)} \over {2x}} - {d \over {dx}}(x)\,.\,{{f(x)} \over x}$$

$$ = 2\,.\,{{f(2x)} \over {2x}} - 1\,.\,{{f(x)} \over x}$$

$$ = {{f(x)} \over x} - {{f(x)} \over x}$$ [as f(2x) = f(x)]

$$ = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ $$g'(x) = 0 \Rightarrow g(x) = $$ constant function

content: A bulb is placed at the centre of a circular track of radius 10 m. A vertical wall is erected touching the track at a point P. A man is running along the track with a speed of 10 m/sec. Starting from P the speed with which his shadow is running along the wall when he is at an angular distance of 60$$^\circ$$ from P is
explanation:
Given, radius of circular track is 10 m.


and speed of man = 10 m/sec. i.e. $$v = r{{d\theta } \over {dt}} = 10$$

Now, $$\tan \theta = {y \over r}$$

$$ \Rightarrow y = r\tan \theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow {{dy} \over {dt}} = r{\sec ^2}\theta \,.\,{{d\theta } \over {dt}}$$

$$ = {\sec ^2}\theta \,.\,r{{d\theta } \over {dt}}$$

$$ = {\sec ^2}60^\circ \,.\,(10)$$

$$ = {(2)^2} \times 10$$

$$ = 40$$ m/sec.

content: Let f(x) > 0 for all x and f'(x) exists for all x. If f is the inverse function of h and $${h'(x) = {1 \over {1 + \log x}}}$$. Then, f'(x) will be
explanation:
Given,

h{f(x)} = x

differentiating w.r.t.x, we get

h'{f(x)} . f'(x) = 1

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$f'(x) = {1 \over {h'\{ f(x)\} }}$$

$$ \Rightarrow f'(x) = {1 \over {{1 \over {1 + \log \{ f(x)\} }}}}$$

$$ \because $$ $$\left[ {h'(x) = {1 \over {1 + \log x}}} \right]$$ (given)

$$ \Rightarrow f'(x) = 1 + \log (f(x))$$

content: Let f(x) be a derivable function, f'(x) > f(x) and f(0) = 0. Then,

options.content: no sign of f(x) can be ascertained

explanation:
Let $$g(x) = {e^{ - x}}f(x)$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$g'(x) = {e^x}f'(x) - {e^{ - x}}f(x)$$

$$ = {e^{ - x}}(f'(x) - f(x))$$

Since, $$f'(x) > f(x)$$, so $$f'(x) - f(x) > 0$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${e^{ - x}}(f'(x) - f(x)) > 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow g'(x) > 0$$

$$ \therefore $$ g(x) is an increasing function.

Now, $$g(x) > g(0)\forall x > 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {e^{ - x}}f(x) > {e^0}f(0)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {e^{ - x}}f(x) > 0$$ [$$ \because $$ $$f(0) = 0$$]

$$ \Rightarrow f(x) > 0,\forall x > 0$$

options.content: f(x) has infinitely many zeroes in (1, 2) for all c
f(x) has exactly one zero in (1, 2) if $$-$$1 < c < 0
f(x) has double zeroes in (1, 2) if $$-$$1 < c < 0
whatever be the value of c, f(x) has no zero in (1, 2)

explanation:
Given,

$$f(x) = {x^4} - 4{x^3} + 4{x^2} + c$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$f(1) = 1 + c$$

and $$f(2) = {2^4} - 4{(2)^3} + 4{(2)^2} + c$$

$$ = 16 - 32 + 16 + c$$

$$ = c$$

Now, $$f(1)\,.\,f(2) = c(1 + c)$$

Let $$f(1)\,.\,f(2) < 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow c(1 + c) < 0 \Rightarrow c \in ( - 1,0)$$

$$ \therefore $$ For $$c \in ( - 1,0)$$, f(x) has exactly one zero is (1, 2).

We have, $${f_1}(x) = {e^x}$$

$${f_2}(x) = {e^{{f_1}(x)}}$$

$${f_{n + 1}}(x) = {e^{{f_n}(x)}}$$

Now, $${f_n}(x) = {e^{{f_{n - 1}}(x)}}$$

On taking log both sides, we get

$$\log \left\{ {{f_n}(x)} \right\} = {f_{n - 1}}(x)\log e$$

$$ \Rightarrow {d \over {dx}}\log \left( {{f_n}(x)} \right) = {d \over {dx}}{f_{n - 1}}(x)$$ ($$ \because $$ log e = 1)

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over {{f_n}(x)}}{d \over {dx}}{f_n}(x) = {f_{n - 1}}'(x)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {d \over {dx}}{f_n}(x) = {f_n}(x){f_{n - 1}}'(x)$$ .... (i)

Now, $${f_1}'(x) = {e^x}$$

and $${f_2}(x) = {e^{{f_1}(x)}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \log {f_2}(x) = {f_1}(x)\log e = {f_1}(x)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over {{f_2}(x)}}.{f_2}'(x) = {f_1}'(x)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {f_2}'(x) = {f_2}(x).{f_1}'(x)$$

$$ = {f_2}(x).{e^x}$$ ($$ \because $$ $${f_1}'(x) = {e^x}$$)

$$ = {f_2}(x).{f_1}(x)$$ [$$ \because $$ $${e^x} = {f_1}(x)$$] ....(ii)

From Eq. (i),

$${d \over {dx}}{f_n}(x) = {f_n}(x).{f_{n - 1}}(x)....{f_1}(x)$$ [using Eq. (ii)]

content: The equation x log x = 3 $$-$$ x
explanation:
Let f(x) = x log x + x $$-$$ 3

$$ \Rightarrow $$ f'(x) = x . $${1 \over x}$$ + log x + 1

$$ \Rightarrow $$ f'(x) = log x + 2 > 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ f(1) = $$-$$ 2 and f(3) = 3 log 3, f(1) . f(3) < 0

Hence, exactly one root in x$$ \in $$ (1, 3) as f(x) > 0

content: If $$f(x) = {\log _5}{\log _3}x$$, then f'(e) is equal to
explanation:
We have,

$$f(x) = {\log _5}{\log _3}x$$

$$ = {{\log {{\log }_3}x} \over {\log 5}}$$

$$ = {{\log \left( {{{\log x} \over {\log 3}}} \right)} \over {\log 5}}$$

$$ = {{\log \log x - \log \log 3} \over {\log 5}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$f'(x) = {1 \over {\log 5}}.{1 \over {\log x}}.{1 \over x}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$f'(e) = {1 \over {e\log 5.\log e}}$$

$$ = {1 \over {e\log 5}}$$ [$$ \because $$ log e = 1]

$$ = {1 \over {e{{\log }_e}5}}$$

content: Let $$f(x) = {x^m}$$, m being a non-negative integer. The value of m so that the equality $$f'(a + b) = f'(a) + f'(b)$$ is valid for all a, b > 0 is
explanation:
We have, $$f(x)=x^m$$

$$\begin{aligned} f(a+b) & =(a+b)^m \\ f^{\prime}(a+b) & =m(a+b)^{m-1} \\ f^{\prime}(a) & =m(a)^{m-1} \text { and } f^{\prime}(b)=m b^{m-1} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Also, } \quad f^{\prime}(a+b) & =f^{\prime}(a)+{ f}^{\prime}(b) \\ m(a+b)^{m-1} & =m a^{m-1}+m b^{m-1} \\ (a+b)^{m-1} & =a^{m-1}+b^{m-1} \end{aligned}$$

From the binomial expansion, we can say that

$$m-1=1 \Rightarrow m=2$$

and $$d$$ false for $$n=1$$ and $$n=3$$

Also, for $$n=0, f(x)=1$$

So, $$f^{\prime}(x)=0 ; f^{\prime}(a+b)=0$$

So, $$f^{\prime}(a+b)=f^{\prime}(a)+f^{\prime}(b)$$

Hence, value of $$m$$ are 0 and 2.

content: Let f and g be differentiable on the interval I and let a, b $$ \in $$ I, a < b. Then,

options.content: If f(a) = 0 = f(b), the equation f'(x) + f(x)g'(x) = 0 is soluble in (a, b)
If f(a) = 0 = f(b), the equation f'(x) + f(x)g'(x) = 0 may not be soluble in (a, b)
If g(a) = 0 = g(b), the equation g'(x) + kg(x) = 0 is soluble in (a, b), k $$ \in $$ R
If g(a) = 0 = g(b), the equation g'(x) + kg(x) = 0 may not be soluble in (a, b), k $$ \in $$ R

explanation:
From option (a)

We have,

$$f(a) = f(b) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow f'(a)\,.\,f'(b) < 0$$

Again, let $$h(x) = f'(x) + f(x)g'(x)$$

$$ \Rightarrow h(a) = f'(a) + f(a)g'(a) = f'(a)$$

and $$h(b) = f'(b) + f(b)g'(b) = f'(b)$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$h(a)\,.\,h(b) = f'(a)\,.\,f'(b) < 0$$

$$ \therefore $$ h(x) = 0 has root between (a, b)

From option (c)

We have, $$g(a) = g(b) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow g'(a)\,.\,g'(b) < 0$$

Again, let $$m(x) = g'(x) + kg(x)$$

$$ \Rightarrow m(a) = g'(a) + kg(a) = g'(a)$$

$$ \Rightarrow m(b) = g'(b) + kg(b) = g'(b)$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$m(a)\,.\,m(b) = g'(a)\,.\,g'(b) < 0$$

$$ \therefore $$ m(x) = 0 has root between (a, b).

So, option (a) and (c) are correct.

content: If f(x) = x<sup>n</sup>, being a non-negative integer, then the values of n for which f'($$\alpha$$ + $$\beta$$) = f'($$\alpha$$) + f'($$\beta$$) for all $$\alpha$$, $$\beta$$ > 0 is
explanation:
We have,

f(x) = xn

$$ \Rightarrow $$ f'(x) = nxn$$-$$1

Now, f'($$\alpha$$ + $$\beta$$) = f'($$\alpha$$) + f'($$\beta$$)

$$ \Rightarrow $$ n($$\alpha$$ + $$\beta$$)n$$-$$1 = n$$\alpha$$n$$-$$1 + n$$\beta$$n$$-$$1

$$ \Rightarrow $$ ($$\alpha$$ + $$\beta$$)n$$-$$1 = $$\alpha$$n$$-$$1 + $$\beta$$n$$-$$1

From options we see that n = 2, satisfy the above equation.

$$ \therefore $$ n = 2
